title: Detecting Cycles in an Undirected Graph: Fix the Low Link Values

statement.markdown:
Write a function to detect whether a given undirected graph has a cycle. If the graph is cyclic, return the node with the lowest lowlink value.

Note that the graph is represented as a list of edges, where each edge is a pair of nodes. The nodes are numbered from 0 to n-1.

This problem is a variation of the original DFS cycle detection problem. Here, we are asked to return the node with the lowest lowlink value if the graph has a cycle, instead of just detecting the presence of a cycle.

Assume that the graph is connected, i.e., there is a path between any two nodes in the graph.

You can use the DFS traversal algorithm to solve this problem.

**Input Formats:** 

* The first line contains an integer n, the number of nodes in the graph.
* The next n-1 lines contain pairs of nodes that form an edge.

**Output Format:** 

* If the graph has a cycle, return the node with the lowest lowlink value.
* If the graph does not have a cycle, return -1.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, the number of nodes in the graph. The next n-1 lines contain pairs of nodes that form an edge.
statement.outputFormat: Return the node with the lowest lowlink value if the graph has a cycle, otherwise return -1.
statement.notes: Assume that the graph is connected, i.e., there is a path between any two nodes in the graph.

hints.text: Recall the definition of lowlink values in the DFS traversal algorithm. How can you use it to detect cycles in a graph?
What should you return if the graph has a cycle? Use the lowlink values to determine which node has the lowest lowlink value.
How can you handle the case when the graph does not have a cycle?

editorial.title: Detecting Cycles in an Undirected Graph: Fix the Low Link Values
editorial.intuition: To detect cycles in a graph, we can use the DFS traversal algorithm. We will use the lowlink values to keep track of the lowest ancestor of each node. If we visit a node and its lowlink value is less than or equal to its discovery time, it means that we have a cycle.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can use a recursive DFS function to traverse the graph. We will keep track of the discovery time and lowlink value for each node. If we visit a node and its lowlink value is less than or equal to its discovery time, it means that we have a cycle. In this case, we will return the node with the lowest lowlink value.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: We can prove the correctness of our solution by showing that it correctly detects cycles in a graph and returns the node with the lowest lowlink value if the graph has a cycle.
editorial.dryRun: The dry run will provide an example of how to use the solution to detect cycles in a graph.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly handling the case when the graph does not have a cycle
Not keeping track of the discovery time and lowlink value for each node

functionSignature.java: int find(int x, int[] disc, int[] low, int[] parent, int timer, int[][] graph);
functionSignature.python: def find(self, x, disc, low, parent, timer, graph):
functionSignature.javascript: var solve = function(x, disc, low, parent, timer, graph);
